content.text: Two constraints govern every agent that reads data from REST APIs at scale: data is delivered in bounded pages, and servers enforce a maximum request rate per client. Understanding these constraints, the mechanisms behind them, and the patterns for handling them correctly is foundational for building agents that reliably complete long-running retrieval tasks without truncated results, 429 errors, or banned API keys.

is the server-side mechanism that splits a large collection of resources into discrete pages, returning one page per response alongside a signal telling the client how to fetch the next one. Instead of returning ten thousand items in a single JSON payload — which would exhaust memory, saturate network buffers, and time out the connection — the server returns a fixed batch (often 20–100 items) with a continuation token or link that the client uses to request subsequent pages. When the continuation signal is absent, the client knows the collection is exhausted.
is the server-side mechanism that caps how many requests a client may send within a rolling or fixed time window. When the client exceeds its quota the server responds with
headers describing the current quota state. Rate limits exist at multiple granularities: per-second burst limits, per-minute sustained limits, and daily quota caps are all common in production APIs.

For agentic systems these two mechanisms intersect. An agent walking a paginated collection of documents, commits, or tickets will issue many sequential requests over a short period. If each page request counts against a rate limit — and they all do — the agent must track its remaining quota as it iterates, slowing or pausing when the budget runs low.

Agents differ from interactive web clients in one critical way: they issue requests in tight automated loops rather than at human pace. A user browsing a GitHub repository might load three pages of issues in five minutes; an agent indexing the same repository for a RAG pipeline might request five hundred pages in sixty seconds. This mismatch makes the failure modes severe: a poorly written agent will hit a rate limit, raise an uncaught exception, lose all pagination state, and restart from page one — issuing another burst of requests, triggering another 429, and so on in a denial-of-service loop that gets the API key suspended.

Pagination correctness is equally important. An agent that stops iterating when it receives an empty page — rather than checking for the absence of a continuation token — will silently miss thousands of records if the API returns an empty intermediate page due to a filter or deleted item. Incomplete data causes downstream failures that are difficult to diagnose because the agent terminates without an error.
Together, correct pagination and proactive rate-limit handling are what separate a brittle agent prototype from a production-grade data pipeline.
— iterating all pages guarantees the agent sees the full dataset, not just the first server-imposed batch. For RAG ingestion or compliance scanning, partial data is equivalent to no data.
— processing one page at a time keeps resident memory proportional to page size, not collection size. An agent can walk a million-item catalog with a fixed 100-item buffer.
— cursor tokens act as checkpoints. Persisting the last cursor after each page means a crashed or interrupted agent can resume from where it stopped, not from the beginning.
— respecting rate limits keeps the API key in good standing. Providers track abusive clients and will temporarily or permanently suspend keys that repeatedly violate limits.
— rate limits on shared APIs protect all clients. An agent that voluntarily throttles its request rate avoids crowding out other users and other agents in a multi-tenant system.
— a proactive token-bucket throttle on the client side converts 429 surprises into smooth pacing, making the total wall-clock time for a large retrieval job predictable and plannable.
Offset pagination is unstable on live data

— if items are inserted or deleted while the agent is iterating, row offsets shift. Pages will skip some items and duplicate others. The stale-data problem is invisible and uncorrectable at the client side.

— once the server issues a cursor for page N, the client cannot jump directly to page N+10. Traversal is always sequential. For use cases that need "jump to page 50" semantics, cursor pagination is the wrong choice.
— a 30-second Retry-After on a rate-limited request directly increases the total job duration. For agents with tight SLAs, rate limit budget must be allocated explicitly during task planning.
— GitHub, Stripe, Salesforce, and Notion each publish different rate limit headers with different semantics and reset windows. There is no standard; every API wrapper must be adapted individually.

— some APIs invalidate cursors after a TTL (commonly 5–30 minutes). An agent that pauses too long between pages will receive a CURSOR_EXPIRED error and must restart from the beginning or from a persisted checkpoint.
The server exposes two query parameters:
(number of rows to skip). The client increments the offset by the page size on each request. The server executes a SQL-style
query. Termination is signaled either by receiving fewer items than the requested limit, or by an explicit

field in the response that the client tracks. This approach is simple to implement and supports random access, but suffers from instability on datasets that change between requests — inserting one row shifts every subsequent page by one.

field alongside each page. The cursor encodes the position in the dataset (typically the ID or timestamp of the last item returned, sometimes base64-encoded). The client passes the cursor back as a query parameter on the next request. The server decodes it to resume from the correct position. Because the cursor tracks an item identifier rather than a row number, insertions above the cursor position do not affect the traversal — the client always sees items it has not yet seen. The Stripe API, GitHub GraphQL, and Notion API all use cursor pagination.
The GitHub REST API and many RFC 5988-compliant APIs embed pagination URLs in the
response header rather than in the JSON body. The header contains labelled URL relations:
URL on each iteration; the absence of a
link signals the end of the collection. The
library exposes parsed link relations via
Three algorithms are common in production API servers. Understanding them helps predict how the limit behaves under different request patterns.
: the server maintains a virtual bucket with capacity
per second up to the capacity. Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the server returns 429. The token count at time
. This algorithm allows short bursts up to capacity
while enforcing a long-run average rate of
requests per second. Most consumer APIs (GitHub, OpenAI, Anthropic) use token buckets.

: incoming requests are queued; the queue drains at a fixed rate. If the queue overflows, requests are dropped with 429. Unlike the token bucket, leaky bucket produces a perfectly smooth output rate regardless of input bursts — it is used where predictable server load matters more than burst tolerance.

: the server counts requests in a rolling time window (e.g., the last 60 seconds). When the count exceeds the limit, subsequent requests are rejected until old requests fall outside the window. Stripe's default rate limiting uses this model.
Providers expose different headers, but the common ones follow a recognizable pattern:
— requests left in the current window
— Unix timestamp when the window resets and the full quota is restored
— seconds to wait before the next request is accepted (present on 429 responses)

The diagram below shows both concerns side by side: the cursor-pagination loop on the left and the rate-limit recovery cycle on the right. The token bucket illustration demonstrates how refill and consumption interact to determine when a client receives a 429.

attrs.alt: Pagination and rate limiting agent request flow diagram

content.text: Proactive throttling — reading x-ratelimit-remaining before each request and slowing down when it approaches zero — prevents 429s entirely. Reactive backoff (sleeping after a 429) is the fallback, not the primary strategy.
Pagination applies whenever the API response includes a continuation signal — a
field — regardless of whether the agent needs all results or just the first matching page. The distinction is in how many pages to consume.
: RAG ingestion, compliance audits, bulk export, and migration jobs — iterate all pages unconditionally.
: search agents retrieve the first page, evaluate relevance, and request a second page only if the first page was insufficient. Early stopping conserves rate limit budget.
: always prefer cursor pagination when the API offers it. Use offset/limit only for static or slowly-changing datasets where stability guarantees are acceptable.
: any agent that calls an API more than a handful of times per session should implement proactive throttling. The cost is a few lines of code; the benefit is stable, uninterrupted runs.
: SDK clients for Stripe, GitHub, and Anthropic wrap pagination in a single iterator. Use the SDK iterator rather than reimplementing the loop from scratch.

The implementation has three separable concerns: a pagination iterator that abstracts the page loop, a rate-limit-aware request function that handles 429s and proactive throttling, and a checkpoint mechanism that persists the cursor so the job can be resumed after interruption.

"""Pagination helpers: cursor, offset/limit, and Link-header styles."""
from __future__ import annotations
import re
import time
from typing import Generator
import requests


# ── Cursor pagination ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def iter_cursor_pages(
    session: requests.Session,
    url: str,
    cursor_field: str = "next_cursor",
    items_field: str = "items",
    **params,
) -> Generator[list[dict], None, None]:
    """Yield each page of items; stop when cursor_field is absent or None."""
    cursor: str | None = None
    while True:
        p = {**params}
        if cursor:
            p["cursor"] = cursor
        resp = session.get(url, params=p, timeout=15)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        yield data.get(items_field, [])
        cursor = data.get(cursor_field)
        if not cursor:
            break


# ── Offset/limit pagination ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def iter_offset_pages(
    session: requests.Session,
    url: str,
    page_size: int = 100,
    items_field: str = "items",
    **params,
) -> Generator[list[dict], None, None]:
    """Yield each page; stop when a page is smaller than page_size."""
    offset = 0
    while True:
        resp = session.get(
            url, params={**params, "limit": page_size, "offset": offset}, timeout=15
        )
        resp.raise_for_status()
        items: list[dict] = resp.json().get(items_field, [])
        if not items:
            break
        yield items
        if len(items) < page_size:
            break
        offset += len(items)


# ── Link-header pagination (GitHub REST style) ────────────────────────────────

def _parse_next_link(link_header: str) -> str | None:
    for part in link_header.split(","):
        url_part, *rels = part.strip().split(";")
        if any('rel="next"' in r for r in rels):
            return url_part.strip().strip("<>")
    return None


def iter_link_pages(
    session: requests.Session,
    url: str,
    **params,
) -> Generator[list, None, None]:
    """Yield each page; stop when there is no Link: rel=next header."""
    next_url: str | None = url
    first = True
    while next_url:
        resp = session.get(next_url, params=params if first else None, timeout=15)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        first = False
        yield resp.json()
        next_url = _parse_next_link(resp.headers.get("Link", ""))

The client-side token bucket enforces a maximum request rate before sending the request. This is proactive throttling — it never sends a request that would immediately be rejected, and it does not rely on receiving a 429 to learn the limit. The 429-backoff wrapper is the reactive fallback for cases where the server's bucket state differs from the client's model (e.g., another process is sharing the same API key).

"""Rate-limit aware request wrapper with proactive throttling and 429 backoff."""
from __future__ import annotations
import random
import time
import requests


class TokenBucketThrottle:
    """Client-side token bucket to pace outbound requests.

    Args:
        rate: sustained requests per second allowed
        burst: maximum burst size (default: 3 × rate)
    """

    def __init__(self, rate: float, burst: float | None = None) -> None:
        self._rate = rate
        self._capacity = burst if burst is not None else rate * 3
        self._tokens = float(self._capacity)
        self._last_refill = time.monotonic()

    def _refill(self) -> None:
        now = time.monotonic()
        elapsed = now - self._last_refill
        self._tokens = min(self._capacity, self._tokens + elapsed * self._rate)
        self._last_refill = now

    def acquire(self) -> None:
        """Block until one token is available."""
        while True:
            self._refill()
            if self._tokens >= 1.0:
                self._tokens -= 1.0
                return
            deficit = (1.0 - self._tokens) / self._rate
            time.sleep(deficit)


def request_with_retry(
    session: requests.Session,
    method: str,
    url: str,
    throttle: TokenBucketThrottle | None = None,
    max_retries: int = 5,
    **kwargs,
) -> requests.Response:
    """Send a request with proactive throttling and exponential backoff on 429."""
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        if throttle:
            throttle.acquire()
        resp = session.request(method, url, timeout=15, **kwargs)
        if resp.status_code != 429:
            resp.raise_for_status()
            return resp
        # Reactive backoff: respect Retry-After with jitter
        retry_after = float(resp.headers.get("Retry-After", 2 ** attempt))
        jitter = random.uniform(0, retry_after * 0.1)
        wait = retry_after + jitter
        time.sleep(wait)
    resp.raise_for_status()   # final attempt failed
    return resp  # unreachable but satisfies type checker


# ── Proactive throttling from response headers ─────────────────────────────────

def maybe_slow_down(resp: requests.Response, throttle: TokenBucketThrottle) -> None:
    """Reduce the throttle rate when remaining quota is low."""
    remaining = resp.headers.get("x-ratelimit-remaining")
    reset = resp.headers.get("x-ratelimit-reset")
    if remaining is None or reset is None:
        return
    remaining_n = int(remaining)
    reset_at = float(reset)
    seconds_to_reset = max(1.0, reset_at - time.time())
    if remaining_n < 10:
        # Conservative: spread remaining budget evenly over reset window
        safe_rate = remaining_n / seconds_to_reset
        throttle._rate = max(0.1, safe_rate)
Simple: collecting all items from a cursor-paginated endpoint

import requests

session = requests.Session()
session.headers["Authorization"] = "Bearer sk-..."

all_docs: list[dict] = []
for page in iter_cursor_pages(session, "https://api.example.com/docs", limit=50):
    all_docs.extend(page)

print(f"Retrieved {len(all_docs)} documents")
Realistic: paginated GitHub issue search with rate limiting

import os
import requests
from pathlib import Path
import json

GH_TOKEN = os.environ["GITHUB_TOKEN"]


def fetch_all_open_issues(owner: str, repo: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Fetch every open issue in a GitHub repository, respecting rate limits."""
    session = requests.Session()
    session.headers.update({
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {GH_TOKEN}",
        "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json",
        "X-GitHub-Api-Version": "2022-11-28",
    })
    throttle = TokenBucketThrottle(rate=5.0, burst=10.0)  # GitHub: 5000/hr ≈ 1.4/sec

    issues: list[dict] = []
    for page in iter_link_pages(
        session,
        f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues",
        state="open",
        per_page=100,
    ):
        # Respect rate limit headers proactively
        resp = session.get(
            f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/issues",
            params={"state": "open", "per_page": 1},
        )
        maybe_slow_down(resp, throttle)

        issues.extend(
            {
                "number": iss["number"],
                "title": iss["title"],
                "labels": [lb["name"] for lb in iss["labels"]],
                "created_at": iss["created_at"][:10],
            }
            for iss in page
        )
    return issues


if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = fetch_all_open_issues("anthropics", "anthropic-sdk-python")
    print(f"Total open issues: {len(result)}")

A document-ingestion agent that builds a knowledge base for a RAG system might need to walk an organization's entire Notion database — tens of thousands of pages. Each Notion database query returns up to 100 items and a

when additional pages exist. Notion's API allows approximately 3 requests per second. An unthrottled agent iterating 10,000 items (100 pages) would exhaust the rate limit immediately. The production-grade version combines cursor pagination with a checkpoint file so interrupted runs resume from the last page, and a token bucket throttle to stay within the 3-req/sec limit.

"""Production RAG ingestion loop with cursor checkpointing and throttling."""
from __future__ import annotations
import json
import os
import time
from pathlib import Path
import requests

NOTION_TOKEN = os.environ["NOTION_TOKEN"]
CHECKPOINT_FILE = Path("/var/data/ingestion-checkpoint.json")
DB_ID = os.environ["NOTION_DATABASE_ID"]


def load_checkpoint() -> str | None:
    if CHECKPOINT_FILE.exists():
        return json.loads(CHECKPOINT_FILE.read_text()).get("cursor")
    return None


def save_checkpoint(cursor: str | None) -> None:
    CHECKPOINT_FILE.write_text(json.dumps({"cursor": cursor, "ts": time.time()}))


def ingest_notion_database(embed_fn) -> int:
    """Walk the Notion database and embed each page; return total pages ingested."""
    session = requests.Session()
    session.headers.update({
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {NOTION_TOKEN}",
        "Notion-Version": "2022-06-28",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
    })
    throttle = TokenBucketThrottle(rate=2.5, burst=5.0)  # stay under 3/sec limit

    cursor = load_checkpoint()
    total = 0

    while True:
        payload: dict = {"page_size": 100}
        if cursor:
            payload["start_cursor"] = cursor

        resp = request_with_retry(
            session, "POST",
            f"https://api.notion.com/v1/databases/{DB_ID}/query",
            throttle=throttle,
            json=payload,
        )
        data = resp.json()

        pages = data.get("results", [])
        embed_fn(pages)   # hand off to embedding pipeline
        total += len(pages)

        cursor = data.get("next_cursor")  # None when has_more is False
        save_checkpoint(cursor)           # checkpoint after every page

        if not data.get("has_more"):
            break

    save_checkpoint(None)   # clear checkpoint on successful completion
    return total

Key design choices: the checkpoint is saved after every page (not at the end), so a crash loses at most one page of progress. The throttle uses rate=2.5 rather than 3.0 to leave a 17% safety margin. The
wrapper handles the rare 429 that slips through proactive throttling. On a 10,000-page database this loop completes in approximately 67 minutes at 2.5 req/sec.
Stopping on an empty page rather than on absent cursor

. An API may return an empty items array on an intermediate page if all items were filtered server-side, but still include a non-null next_cursor pointing to the next real page. Stopping on empty items silently skips the rest of the dataset.
Tight retry loop on 429 without exponential backoff

. Retrying immediately after receiving a 429 issues more requests against a bucket that is already empty, producing a cascade of 429s and burning through the retry budget. Always wait at least the Retry-After duration, and add jitter to prevent multiple concurrent agents from synchronizing their retries.

. Spawning ten concurrent threads each iterating a different paginated endpoint against the same API key multiplies the request rate by ten. The total rate must stay under the limit regardless of how many concurrent paginators exist.

. Using offset/limit on a database that is actively receiving inserts causes pages to shift. Items near the boundary between two pages are either returned twice or skipped entirely. The fix is to use cursor-based pagination, or to sort by a stable, non-changing field such as creation timestamp.

. A long ingestion job without checkpoints restarts from page one on any failure — network timeout, OOM, deployment restart. On large datasets this wastes hours of compute and re-issues thousands of duplicate API calls.

. Many agents only react to 429s. Reading x-ratelimit-remaining before each request and slowing down when the budget is nearly depleted is far less disruptive than hitting the wall and waiting 30–60 seconds for reset.
Use the provider's SDK iterator when one exists

. The Stripe Python client's auto_paging_iter(), the GitHub Python SDK's paginate(), and the Anthropic SDK's streaming iterators all implement pagination and basic retry internally. Avoid reimplementing what the SDK provides.
Set a conservative client-side rate below the server limit
. If the published limit is 10 requests/second, set the client throttle to 7–8 to leave headroom for other processes sharing the key and for burst overhead in the token bucket.
Persist the cursor after every page, not at the end
. Writing the cursor to a file or database after each page bounds the loss on failure to one page, regardless of how large the dataset is.
Add random jitter to all backoff sleeps

. When multiple agents or processes all hit the rate limit simultaneously, deterministic backoff (sleep exactly 30s) causes a thundering herd — all agents retry at the same moment. Adding 0–10% random jitter staggers the retries.
Log rate limit header values at debug level
. During development, log x-ratelimit-remaining on every response. This makes it easy to see how quickly the budget depletes and verify that proactive throttling is working.
Separate rate limiters per API key or per tenant

. In multi-tenant systems where different customers have different API keys with different quotas, each key must have its own throttle instance. A shared throttle would incorrectly treat all keys as having the same rate.
The total time for a full-iteration paginated job is bounded by
is the throttle rate in requests per second. For a job with 1,000 pages at a 5 req/sec rate limit:
. The latency floor is set by the rate limit, not the network.

To improve throughput while respecting rate limits: use the maximum page size the API allows (often 100 or 200) to minimize request count for a given item count; stream items to the downstream processor rather than accumulating all pages in memory before processing; and use async I/O with a semaphore-based concurrency limit when multiple independent API calls can be parallelized without exceeding the shared rate budget.
For agents with multiple tools that all call the same API, the rate limit budget is shared across all concurrent tool invocations. A shared
instance at the module level ensures that all tool handlers aggregate their consumption against the same counter.
Several libraries provide pagination and rate-limit utilities so agents do not need to implement everything from scratch.
: the standard approach for Python HTTP with built-in retry on transient errors. Set
: a general retry library with a decorator API.
handles the backoff policy cleanly without manual loops.
: lightweight decorator-based throttling for synchronous code.
: for async agents, an async token bucket and a semaphore limiting concurrent requests are the idiomatic solution.
: the official Stripe, GitHub (PyGitHub or ghapi), Anthropic, and Google API Python client libraries all wrap pagination in iterators and handle 429 backoff. Prefer these over raw HTTP.
Rate limiting and pagination interact with several security and operational concerns that become critical in production:

: providers monitor per-key error rates. A key that regularly receives 429 responses or makes requests after receiving a 429 may be flagged as abusive and temporarily or permanently suspended. Proactive throttling is the primary defense.

: some APIs encode sensitive information in cursor tokens (e.g., the last-seen ID in a customer table). Cursors received from an API must be treated as opaque tokens — do not log them to publicly accessible logs, as they may be usable by another client to probe data ordering.

: in agentic pipelines where the cursor value is derived from user input (e.g., the agent is resuming a task described in a user message), validate that the cursor string is opaque and server-issued, not a crafted value designed to trigger server-side vulnerabilities.

: a buggy API may return a cursor that points back to a page already visited, creating an infinite loop. Add a maximum-pages-per-run limit (e.g., 10,000 pages) as a safeguard. Log a warning and break if the limit is reached.

: when multiple agent processes or workers share the same API key, rate limit consumption must be coordinated. A distributed token bucket backed by Redis is the production solution for multi-process agents.

Think of a REST API as a library with a strict librarian. The library holds millions of books (items), but the librarian will only carry five books at a time (page size). To collect all the books in a section, you hand the librarian a bookmark after each trip, and they use it to know exactly where to resume (cursor). The librarian also enforces a rule: no patron may take more than thirty trips per hour (rate limit). If you exceed that, you are asked to wait in the lobby for a specific duration before returning (Retry-After). The smart patron counts their remaining trips, slows down when they have only a few left, and keeps their bookmark safe so a fire drill does not make them start over from the beginning.
Stop pagination on absent cursor, not on empty items array
— checking the continuation token is the only reliable termination signal.
for any live or frequently-updated dataset — offset pagination produces skips and duplicates on mutation.
— a client-side token bucket that slows down before the rate limit is hit avoids disruptive 30-second pauses.
Always respect Retry-After and add jitter
— ignoring the header or retrying immediately after a 429 worsens the rate limit situation and risks key suspension.
Checkpoint the cursor after every page
— this bounds data loss on failure to one page regardless of total dataset size.
The rate limit budget is global per API key
— all concurrent tools, workers, and processes sharing the same key must share a single throttle instance, whether in-process or via Redis.
Use the provider's SDK iterator when available
— official clients implement pagination, backoff, and retry correctly for their own API. Manual reimplementation duplicates tested logic.
